abstract: Productivity barriers and opportunities influencing smallholder irrigation sustainability in Zimbabwe were identified using case studies of the Silalatshani and Mkoba irrigation schemes. The major barriers were poor infrastructure and soil fertility, and poor access to farm inputs, farm implements, functioning markets and agricultural knowledge, which resulted in low yields, food insecurity and negative farm income. Most irrigated land remains unused, and marketing of produce is uncoordinated. Mobile technologies provide opportunities for market information dissemination. Institutions are needed to continuously encourage dialogue among agricultural value chain stakeholders to allow irrigators to align their operations to market demands and improve the viability of irrigation systems.
funders: Australian Centre for International Agricultural Research (ACIAR)
publication: International Journal of Water Resources Development

documents.formatdesc: It is an Open Access article

affiliation: School of Commerce, University of South Australia (Adelaide)
keywords: Productivity barriers and opportunities, Smallholder irrigation systems, Sustainability, Irrigation productivity, Zimbabwe
note:
This work was supported by the Australian Centre for International Agricultural Research [grant no.
FSC2013-006].
title: Irrigation development in Zimbabwe: understanding productivity barriers and opportunities at Mkoba and Silalatshani irrigation schemes
